speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 2 of the interview with Jacob Rosenberg being conducted in Melbourne, Australia, on the 9th of September, 1996. Jacob, how old were you when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what memories do you have of hearing about the war breaking out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that that the wars is imminent, although we did not believe. It is funny how people are. We would not believe because we just could not accustom ourselves that, that another war is coming. My parents still remembered the First World War when there's another catastrophe. And then I've seen the mobilization. And I looked at the Polish Army. And I thought, they invincible. They invincible. I looked at those-- at the cavalry and the, the funny guns they used to carry. And I thought, this is-- this is the ultimate in, in, in weaponry. And it took them about seven days. And Poland was defeated, except for Warsaw, which stood up against, against Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So the war-- the actual war was, was so swift that you didn't even have a chance to, to contemplate what's going to happen. Except in the second or third day of, of the invasion, France and England declared war on Germany. And we thought, this is the end of Germany. Germany is kaput because France and England, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We really didn't understood what's going to be there. We had little knowledge, you know about the military strength of Germany and about the unpreparedness of England and France and the whole Western world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What, what also made it devastating is a lot of Jewish people were left-orientated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They believed in Soviet Russia. This was not, most probably, a, a, a conscious attitude of idealism. This was not anything to do with Jewish belief. And, and-- but with the economical and anti-Semitic situation prevalent in Poland, we thought that Russia is, is something different. It's not-- you see, the lie and the secrecy about Soviet Russia was so great, we could not-- we could not believe that they actually can make a pact with a country like, like Germany. And all of a sudden, we, we were confronted with a new reality, a devastating reality.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your life change then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it changed in that-- and to that effect that after six days of war, my family was totally destituted. We had no food. We had no money. We had no work. Everything was in a chaos. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was difficult, very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father couldn't do a thing because there was-- it was murderous for men to go out in the street, you know, they-- terrible. So my mother took-- plucked up some courage. And she went to the country and took a few things with her, some-- I can't remember what-- some garments, some clothes, frocks and things. And she exchanged it for food. And she brought home some food. And we had that. And then, then, it become, so, so to speak, normalized-- life became normalized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was living with you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived altogether. On top of it, my older sister had a little girl. She came back. She was married, but she came back to stay with us. And unfortunately, of course, she was political, non-kosher, so to speak, before the war. When the Germans marched in, they find all the-- all the necessary papers, who was a communist, who was-- who was a political instigator. And they came up to her-- because she was arrested when she was a young girl, she-- they came up to our home. And they arrested her from my home on, on-- on January, 1940, she was arrested. And I remember two Gestapos agents came in. And her little girl, [INAUDIBLE], she began to cry, to lament. My mother, they're taking away my mother! And one of the Gestapo agents said to her, you stop crying because your mother is coming back soon, within next hundred years. Now my-- this little niece couldn't understand what he meant by hundred years. So she, she was-- she's coming back soon. She came back later on. She came back illegally to get her. She smuggled herself through to see her little child, to be with the child. And her husband escaped to Soviet Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was her child at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her child was two years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My other-- my other sister and my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your other sister have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, she had a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: What anti-Jewish actions took place once the war had broken out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [LAUGHS] this was constant-- constantly harassment. It was a real policy, you know, not to let us live, not to let us walk on the street. 5 o'clock, we had to be at home. We couldn't go out from the house before 9 o'clock in the morning. And, and Jews were not permitted to hold any office or that you're not permitted to, to work, not to practice medicine or law, or anything, you know. They nothing. All bank accounts were closed. Any money you had in the bank, you couldn't use. All businesses were, were, were taken away from Jewish people. It-- that made it impossible for us to continue to, to live like human beings. And their plan was to, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to bring us together on specific spots. And they create ghetto. And from ghetto-- and from ghettos, you know, took-- gradually take us out to camps, to concentration camps, to, to annihilation camps, to extermination camps. This was the German, German policy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do prior to the ghetto being created?

speaker: interviewee
text: We haven't done it-- not much. We haven't done it. Ah, as--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we live-- my father was a-- we were trying to sell whatever we could, you know, whatever we had that were in our possessions. We saved constantly. And my father made it-- with that money, we established a cleaning business. And they cleaned things from, from other people who could, could afford more money. It was not life. It was-- that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was communal life affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first stages, it was no communal life. But later on, we reorganized. We regrouped later on as the ghetto was established.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the time leading up to the establishment of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I just told you. I just mentioned it to you that the-- it, it-- everything went so swift that the-- all these harassments which I spoke about and, and this sort of constant infringement on our-- on our freedom, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this was part of a plan. The establishment of the ghetto, the establishment-- a hierarchy of, of Jews who will be able to cooperate with the Germans, this is of one-- you know, one of the most important aspect of the Holocaust, as far as the Germans were concerned, is to annihilate the Jews in secrecy and orderly. The victim are not supposed to know till the last moment that he is going to annihilated. And order has to be. And for that, they, they found their stooges hidden amongst our people, like-- I mean, like Chaim Rumkowski, who collaborated with them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One cannot-- it would be false to accuse to say that he knew exactly, in, in, in the initial stages what is taking place when we are taking from ghetto. But later on, he knew. And he still collaborated. He knew. And he thought that he's going to outsmarten them. It was stupid. A big egomaniac, a fool who is responsible, who took, took on himself a responsibility, which we say, should not forgive-- the Jewish people should not forgive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the ghetto established?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you mean-- what do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they, they, they-- one day, they start building up fences, barbed wires, and they-- like a huge cage. We didn't know what this is all about. We thought this just to protect against traffic or military movement. All of a sudden, we got up one morning, we, we-- we're in an enclosure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain in the-- living in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. We lived in the same place. And the ghetto was closed on the 1st of May, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were people brought in from anywhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from the city people were brought in. And those who the-- were not high income, they find ways how to do it, the Germans-- they shoot a few hundred of them. And everybody took off in a panic. They just-- on one morning, they went, and they murdered whoever was sort of-- whoever they feel like, get them out of the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What security existed in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what sense?

speaker: interviewer
text: If people could leave, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: People couldn't leave. You couldn't get out from ghetto. To step out of the ghetto was-- is dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did all the people who had arrived in the ghetto go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they pushed them into wherever they could. We lived about 10 or 20 people in a room. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You had no say over your-- over your premises.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear any-- anything that distinguished--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Initially, when there, there, we had the armbands. But later on, we had this yellow patch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you wear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: On, on my right-hand breast-- chest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what you felt when you were given the yellow patch?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, it was a setback for a young man to wear this, sort of. But then I-- right through the war, I never had this feeling of being inferior to them who wore the gun. I never felt it. And I remembered that before the war in 1938, there was a play staged in Lodz. I can't remember by whom. It was the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when I put on that yellow patch, that play came back to my-- to me like a, a reflex, that all of a sudden, I'm taken back to the Middle Ages. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I did not feel the shame of it. I, I felt, rather, they should feel, feel ashamed of making people putting on yellow stars, yellow patches. I, I, I, I-- not even shame-- I thought this-- it's, it's a, a stupid move. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It does not belong. It does not belong in, in, in our society, amongst people. Despite all the anti-Semitisms, despite everything which took place, I taught this is out of context.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for food in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans realized-- they already had a plan to transform this ghetto them into a giant-- gigantic, industrial center [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they can get things made perfectly well-- beautiful things made for no money. They don't have to pay. They just get to give a piece of bread and a-- and a thin soup, a potato or a vegetable. And they work. And that's, that's what took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in a clothing factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: --making uniforms for the German Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the other members in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They also were involved, all in German industry. They made millions. Billions they took out from, from, from, from our sweat and work, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes they talk about this restitution money we're getting now. It is so stupid. Even they-- even they pay you-- even they would pay $1,000 a day now for the rest of my life, they couldn't repay what-- the work we did and, and, and, and the conditions, and without food and without-- you know, sometimes people don't understand. He said, he was a worker. What means a worker in ghetto? You were-- you were not a worker in ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had no holiday. You had no-- [LAUGHS] you had no freedom. You had to-- you, you got started off at 8 o'clock in the morning, and you finished 6 o'clock in the evening, 10 hours nonstop, a half-an-hour break for a bit of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soup, this water soup. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what, what? Is this a-- working? Like Primo, Primo Levi said in his book is, is this is man? Should this be men? We were workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not workers. We were just slaves, even worse than slaves. Because even for a slave, a master has responsibility. They have not responsibility for us. They want us to die. For-- but a matter of fact, if one was opposing their wishes to-- for us to die, we rebelled against them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's got to be looked in this context, that a Jew who did not pick up a gun to fight the Germans but he protected himself to live was a rebellious man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Was there a special place you went to collect food? Or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they had these sort of-- called food shops.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have cards or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, cards. We had cards-- ration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who used to go to collect the food for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob Rosenberg. I was the youngest and was probably the strongest in the family. And I wouldn't let my father go. And my mother couldn't go and, of course, my sisters. So I was the one who collected it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you carry everybody's coupon?

speaker: interviewee
text: I carried everybody's coupon, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep warm in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: By thinking about fire. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories of winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible memories. My, my poetry is full of snow and frost. It left on me an indelible mark. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember our room-- the icicles hanging from the ceiling. On top of paperwork was brilliant snow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our stove was like a tombstone, our saucepan like open hungry mouths, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and our table without bread. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a piece of bread locked in a cupboard, which my mother kept under lock so nobody touches. When she's decided-- when we-- she felt that, that's it and we don't go to bed hungry, she divided that piece of bread amongst equal portions that at least in the night we can sleep through the night. Because I had many times members of my family cry in the night from hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, to talk about hunger in Australia sounds a little bit outlandish. What are you talking, that guy? Hunger? There's no food? No bread? But that's how it was. How much were your rations? What, what would you eat in a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I really can't remember that-- just to keep you barely alive. I can't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever remember the rations being decreased?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were decreased. And sometimes, if they had a, a, a oversupply of potatoes, we had a little bit more, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about in summer? What was it like in the ghetto in summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Summer? A lot of dust, no rain, you know. But in one of my poems I describe how we worked it out that people found a new way. They, they had little plots. And they cultivated vegetables as a supplement. It helped, helped a great deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to read that poem for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is dealing-- actually, this is, is dealing with, with the story about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] relating what's the Holocaust? How do you describe it? What do you talk? How, how can you-- in one of my poems, I, I, I plead with my master. I said, Master, grant me a language, no, grant me the words of an illogical language, how fathers used to murder little children and ask the heavenly-- their god-- to bless their-- bless their work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now all this sort of thing is very complicated. And, and, and therefore, I believe greatly in, in describing what took place in, in a epic form. Why in epic form?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the metaphor which is used in poetry is not to embellish. It's not to, to dramatize. But it's-- it is the magnifying glass through which one can see what really took place, that one can describe, even with the limited amount of, of language available to us. And the limitation of language stems from the fact, in my opinion, that what took place lies outside the logic of human beings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the logic of-- a human being prepared language as he went alone in history, as we came from, from, from our-- the primordial stages till now. We could describe. We found adjectives and, and, and words to describe things which we were familiar with. This is something we have-- we, we never experience. In one of my-- I, I, I, I say here about-- in the 'Elegy on Ghetto,' I would like to talk-- tell about ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Is there a language for that? How do you describe a child's plea silenced by death? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or how do you explain a man's heart forced to witness his wife beaten, tormented, raped, buried alive? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or how do you tell of a hanging, one noose for father and son? And soldiers stand about the gallows to enjoy the fun.' Now, where do you get this sort of language? Where, where is the mechanism to tell this? Because when I talk about it and write about it, all this comes back to me. All this comes back that a father was forced to hang his son. And the father said, what can I do? And the son said to him, come on. You do it. And his father put his head in because he didn't-- couldn't, couldn't-- he couldn't hang himself. He said, we hang together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, those things are difficult to, to explain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the art of normal-- of normality, I, I-- you've got to be crazy to find language to tell this sort of story. And then I go on to say, 'I resolve to speak in verse. Perhaps it would lessen my pain. Or maybe the verse is my curtain to bleed and sustain.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Why had the murderer spared me? My dear ones, oh God, what an end! Trapped in a bunker like mice, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gassed and burned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Or visions, horrors of horrors, companions of all my night, will I be forever hostage to devilish sights?'

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, those things-- [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] every time I write about it, it's like a pilgrimage back into the dark and darkness. And I know the story has to be told. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know. I really don't know. And, and, and, and language becomes a real obstacle. And one of-- in my latest book, but let me first read you another page on this poem to give you an example what I-- the difficulties. I spoke about my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I spoke about the devilish sights. 'Even in moments of joy, there hides an insidious catch, my father's bleeding fingers mending my yellow patch. My father was a man of peace, a dreamer locked up in his dream, with an open, generous hand, though his pockets were lean. He toiled all his life. But money was not his god. Lest his earned penny bring relief, he would rather choke on his lot.' Well, this gives you more a picture of my father, what sort of man he was. 'And then my mother, my sweet little mother, her story an obvious trial, and yet her eyes spark with hope, her lips a lingering smile. She loved the open space at the early break of dawn, and sung of twilight shadows when this sun was gone. I also had two sisters, mothers in their prime of life-- Ida, the gentle dove, and Pola, the rebel in strife. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they, my two little nieces, [? butts ?] of a miscarried spring, never permitted to play, verboten to sing.'

speaker: interviewer
text: So this gives you more of a picture of ghetto, what-- how my life was. I will not continue with that because this is a long poem. It might take me another 10, 15 minutes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But just to go back to the issue of language. And this poem, in 'No Exit--'

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just coming to the end--
